articles.title: Heatwave relief finally arrives as late-night storm hits Tricity - The Tribune
articles.full_text:
Heatwave takes breather as late-night storm hits Tricity; IMD retains yellow alert till Wednesday
Thunderstorm, rain, gusty winds disrupt power supply in parts of Chandigarh, Mohali and Panchkula
After a week of largely ineffective weather alerts and unrealised rainfall forecasts, parts of Chandigarh, Mohali and Panchkula finally witnessed a spell of much-awaited rain accompanied by thunderstorms, lightning and gusty winds late on Sunday night, bringing temporary relief from the prevailing hot and humid conditions.
The weather change came shortly after the India Meteorological Department (IMD) issued a fresh warning around 10 pm, forecasting thunderstorms, lightning and gusty winds over several districts of Punjab and Haryana, including Mohali and adjoining areas of the Tricity.
Soon after the warning, light to moderate rain accompanied by thunder, lightning and strong gusty winds swept across parts of Chandigarh, Mohali and Panchkula, marking the most significant weather activity in the region since last week’s thunderstorm spell.
The storm disrupted normal life in several areas. Power supply was suspended in multiple sectors and residential localities as gusty winds damaged electricity infrastructure. Reports were received of tree branches snapping, power lines breaking down and unipoles being damaged or uprooted at some places. Several areas also witnessed temporary fluctuations in electricity and water supply as utility agencies rushed to restore services.
The late-night weather activity stood in contrast to the broader trend of the past week, during which repeated yellow and orange alerts for thunderstorms and rain had produced little impact across the Tricity despite active weather systems affecting neighbouring regions.
According to the latest IMD bulletin, Chandigarh recorded a maximum temperature of 39.8 degrees Celsius, 1.2 degrees above the previous day’s reading and 2.3 degrees above normal, while the minimum temperature settled at 25 degrees Celsius, remaining 2.6 degrees below normal. No rainfall had been recorded at the observatory till the evening observation period. The airport observatory recorded a maximum of 40.2 degrees Celsius and a minimum of 27.6 degrees Celsius.
Across Haryana, average maximum temperatures rose by 0.3 degree Celsius and remained 2.3 degrees above normal, with Rohtak recording the state’s highest maximum temperature at 42.1 degrees Celsius. Night temperatures also rose marginally across the region.
Despite Sunday’s late-night storm, IMD expects hot and humid conditions to persist over much of Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh during the coming days. A yellow alert for thunderstorms, lightning and gusty winds at isolated places remains in force till Wednesday, with the possibility of scattered showers in some areas.
However, weather officials caution that rainfall activity is likely to remain localized rather than widespread. Similar alerts issued over the past week generated little impact in Chandigarh, Mohali and Panchkula, though isolated pockets elsewhere in Punjab and Haryana witnessed thunderstorm activity.
The forecast indicates partly cloudy skies and isolated rainfall activity through Wednesday, followed by a return to predominantly dry weather conditions from Thursday onwards. No weather warning or alert has been issued beyond Wednesday.
For the Tricity, maximum temperatures are expected to remain in the 36-38 degrees Celsius range, while minimum temperatures are likely to hover between 25 and 27 degrees Celsius over the next several days.
An IMD meteorologist said the late-night thunderstorm was triggered by localised instability and moisture incursion over parts of Punjab and adjoining areas.
“The region continues to remain on the fringe of major weather systems affecting northern India. While localized thunderstorms such as the one witnessed tonight can provide temporary relief, they are unlikely to produce a sustained fall in temperatures unless supported by a larger and more organised weather system. Therefore, hot and humid conditions are expected to continue, though isolated thunderstorms and scattered showers remain possible till midweek,” the meteorologist said.
The latest spell nevertheless provided a welcome break for residents who have watched heavy rain lash Delhi-NCR, Himachal Pradesh and other northern states in recent days while Chandigarh, Mohali and Panchkula remained largely dry. Whether Sunday’s storm marks the beginning of a more active phase or merely another isolated pre-monsoon event will become clearer over the next 48 hours as the yellow alert remains in force across the region.
